levels.title: SOC Level 1
levels.subtitle: Initial Triage & Fundamentals
levels.description: Foundational knowledge, standard SOC workflows, initial alert triage, and basic investigation processes.

node.title: SOC Essentials
node.overview: A SOC is a dedicated, usually 24/7 team (and the operational capability supporting it) responsible for protecting an organisation’s digital assets servers, endpoints, networks, cloud, identity systems and data by continuously monitoring, detecting, investigating, and responding to security incidents.

node.title: What is Incident Response
node.overview: Incident response (IR) is the structured process an organization follows to identify, investigate, contain, and recover from a cybersecurity incident. It combines technical actions, decision-making, coordination and documentation to reduce damage, restore services, and learn from what happened.

node.title: Incident Response Cycle
node.overview: Incident Response (IR) refers to the processes and technologies an organization employs to detect and react to cyber threats, security breaches, or cyber attacks. Implementing a formal incident response plan empowers cyber security teams to mitigate or prevent damage effectively.

node.title: Frameworks
node.overview: Converted from the original Notion export and preserved as part of the Blue Team Study Portal.

node.title: The Hive
node.overview: TheHive is an open-source Security Incident Response Platform (SIRP) designed to centralize incident investigations and case management for SOCs, CSIRTs and CERTs. It enables teams to collect alerts, create investigations (cases), track actions with tasks, enrich observables, and collaborate in real time.

node.title: Important Windows Directories
node.overview: The Security Accounts Manager (SAM) hive stores local user accounts and password hashes.

node.title: Network Defenses
node.overview: Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS) is either a piece of software or a dedicated appliance that inspects network traffic and raises alerts when suspicious or malicious behavior is detected. These alerts are passed on to security analysts for investigation.

node.title: Logging Fundamentals
node.overview: Logging records timestamped events across applications, operating systems, network devices, and cloud services.

node.title: SIEM
node.overview: SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) is a cybersecurity solution that collects, aggregates, normalizes, and analyzes logs and event data from across an organization’s IT infrastructure to detect threats, support incident response, and facilitate forensic investigations.

node.title: ELK (Elastic Stack)
node.overview: Elastic Stack (ELK) is a lightweight SIEM foundation Beats collect, Logstash parses, Elasticsearch indexes, Kibana hunts & dashboards.

node.title: Windows processes
node.overview: Windows processes are programs running in the background that carry out system operations and execute programs or applications on a Windows operating system. Each process has its own memory space and allocated resources. In the Windows environment, various activities such as account logins, file access, memory allocation, running DLLs, and application execut

node.title: Sysinternals
node.overview: Sysinternals is a collection of advanced Windows utilities (70+ tools) originally created by Mark Russinovich and Bryce Cogswell to help troubleshoot, analyze and manage Windows systems. The suite covers file/disk, networking, process, security, system-info and miscellaneous utilities used by sysadmins, developers and security professionals. (Microsoft Learn

node.title: EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response)
node.overview: Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) instruments endpoints (workstations, servers, VMs, cloud workloads, IoT) with a sensor/agent that collects granular telemetry and forwards it to a central console where correlation, ML and rules generate alerts. The EDR also provides response controls (isolate, kill, quarantine, remote shell, artifact collection). EDR evol

levels.description: Advanced investigation, forensics, threat hunting, detection engineering, and analyst tooling.

node.title: Hunting Persistence (MITRE TA0003)
node.overview: Persistence is a crucial technique used by malware authors to maintain access to a compromised system, even after reboots, logoffs, or credential changes. Attackers use various methods to achieve persistence, such as:

node.title: Hunting Lateral Movement (MITRE TA0008)
node.overview: Lateral movement is a critical phase in an attack where adversaries transition from an initially compromised system to other systems within the network. This enables them to escalate privileges, access sensitive information, and expand their foothold throughout an organization. Attackers employ various techniques to achieve lateral movement, such as:

node.title: Hunting Discovery (MITRE TA0007)
node.overview: Discovery (MITRE ATT&CK TA0007) is the set of actions an adversary takes after gaining a foothold to learn the target environment and find the best paths to achieve objectives (privilege escalation, lateral movement, credential access, data access/exfiltration). Discovery includes enumerating users, groups, computers, services, network configuration, shares,

node.title: Memory Forensics
node.overview: Memory forensics is a critical component of digital investigations. It allows analysts to capture and examine volatile system state information that disappears on shutdown. node.title: Stenography
node.overview: Steganography is the practice of hiding messages or information within ordinary, non-secret data.

node.title: Active Directory
node.overview: Before diving into Active Directory (AD), it’s important to understand the role of Directory Services in modern networks. Directory Services provide a structured way to organize, map, and control access to resources within an organization.

node.title: Kerberos authentication
node.overview: Kerberos is a secure authentication protocol used in Windows environments. It uses a ticket system and strong encryption to verify identities, even on open networks. In an Active Directory setup, the Domain Controller acts as the Key Distribution Center (KDC) with two main parts: the Authentication Service (AS) and the Ticket Granting Service (TGS), which is

node.title: Command and Control
node.id: windows security monitoring/windows threat detection 3/command and control
node.overview: You already learned that USB worms and phishing attachments can "infect" the machine. But how do they do it exactly? How do threat actors send the commands and keep control of the victim's host? This task will shed light on this topic and explore the Command and Control (C2) MITRE tactic.
node.relPath: Windows Security Monitoring/Windows Threat Detection 3/Command and Control.md

node.title: Command-and-Control (C2) Evasion Techniques
node.overview: Think of a “command-and-control” (C2) server as the central headquarters of an espionage network. If the malware consistently invoked the same domain or phone number, security teams could quickly identify and neutralize it. To avoid detection, threat actors employ various techniques to ensure their C2 infrastructure does not present as a single, static targe

node.title: Detection Engineering
node.overview: Detection Engineering is the backbone of modern security operations. As threats become more advanced and frequent, reactive security is no longer sufficient. Detection engineering shifts the approach: from generic alerting to precision-crafted, validated detection logic that targets adversarial behavior in context.

node.title: Sigma
node.overview: Sigma is an open-source, generic signature format for log-based detection. It uses YAML to define structured rules, enabling security analysts to detect threats across various SIEMs. Sigma rules specify log sources, conditions, and metadata, making them adaptable to different query languages. Essentially, Sigma standardizes log analysis, much like Snort does

node.title: Atomic Red Team
node.overview: Atomic Red Team is an open-source library of “atomic” tests (small scripts/commands) that simulate individual adversary tactics and techniques mapped to the MITRE ATT&CK framework. Developed by Red Canary and the community, it lets security teams quickly and portably reproduce attacker behaviors to validate defenses. In practice, teams use it to emulate spec

node.title: PowerShell Logs
node.overview: PowerShell logs are often underrated, although they can provide vital insights into threat actors' activity. EDR solutions tend to block common malware, and simple CMD is not powerful enough for exploitation needs. PowerShell is becoming increasingly popular, mainly because of the popularity of so-called fileless attacks. There are three core PowerShell logg

node.title: LOLBins (living-off-the-land binaries)
node.overview: LOLBins (Living Off the Land Binaries) are legitimate, pre-installed executables and scripts—integral parts of the operating system or common software packages—that have normal administrative functions. Their inherent trust (often due to digital signing by the vendor) makes them ideal for attackers who wish to avoid introducing external malicious code. Key c

node.title: Osquery
node.overview: osquery (open-source, originally developed at Facebook, 2014) exposes an endpoint’s runtime state as queryable tables (a relational view of OS artifacts). You use SQL-like SELECT queries to retrieve structured endpoint data — processes, users, network sockets, installed software, registry/startup entries, files and hashes, etc. It runs on Windows, Linux, mac
